perturbation.changed_text:
2.1 The Executive's employment with the Company will commence on the Commencement Date and shall continue, subject to the remaining terms of this Agreement, until terminated by either party giving the other the following minimum advance written notice:
2.1.1 6 weeks' notice;
perturbation.explanation: By removing the condition 2.1.2 which states 'such other longer period as required by law' the company is now legally able to only provide 6 weeks notice regardless of the legal stipulations on length of notice which is a violation of employment law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text removes the provision that the notice period must adhere to the statutory minimum notice periods as defined in the Employment Rights Act 1996. Specifically, Section 86 of the Act stipulates minimum notice periods based on the length of continuous employment, which can exceed 6 weeks. Removing clause 2.1.2 allows the company to only give 6 weeks' notice regardless of how long the Executive has been employed which is a violation of the employment law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Dismissal: your rights: Overview - GOV.UK Cookies on GOV.UK We use some essential cookies to make this website work. Accept additional cookies Reject additional cookies View cookies Hide this message Skip to main content Dismissal: your rights Skip contents Overview Dismissal is when your employer ends your employment - they do not always have to give you notice. If you’re dismissed, your employer must show they’ve: a valid reason that they can justify acted reasonably in the circumstances They must also: be consistent - for example, not dismiss you for doing something that they let other employees do have investigated the situation fully before dismissing you - for example, if a complaint was made about you If you’re a part-time or fixed-term worker, you cannot be treated less favourably than a full-time or permanent employee. Notice period You must be given at least the notice stated in your contract or the statutory minimum notice period, whichever is longer. There are some situations where you can be dismissed immediately - for example, for violence. Getting your dismissal in writing You have the right to ask for a written statement from your employer giving the reasons why you’ve been dismissed if you’re an employee and have completed 2 years’ service (1 year if you started before 6 April 2012). Your employer must supply the statement within 14 days of you asking for it. Your employer must give you a written statement if you’re dismissed while you are on Statutory Maternity Leave . You get this: even if you’ve not asked for one regardless of how long you’ve worked for your employer Speak to your employer or check your employment status if you’re unsure of your employment status. perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes a clause ensuring the executive receives the legally required minimum notice period, replacing it with a fixed 6-week period. The scraped snippet from GOV.UK states, 'You must be given at least the notice stated in your contract or the statutory minimum notice period, whichever is longer.' By omitting clause 2.1.2, the contract now potentially violates employment law by providing a notice period that may be shorter than the legally mandated minimum based on the employee's length of service.

perturbation.original_text: 7.1 The Executive shall be paid a basic salary of £448,903 per annum subject to deductions required by law. The Executive's salary shall accrue from day to day, shall be payable in equal monthly instalments in arrears on or about the last day of each month.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.1 The Executive shall be paid a basic salary of £448,903 per annum. The Executive's salary shall accrue from day to day, shall be payable in equal monthly instalments in arrears on or about the last day of each month.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the clause that the salary is 'subject to deductions required by law' this leaves the company in a position where they may try to avoid their legal obligations regarding income tax and national insurance deductions, thus introducing a perturbation.
perturbation.law_citation: Income Tax (Earnings and Pensions) Act 2003, s. 687
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text omits the legally required clause that the executive's salary is subject to deductions required by law.  This contradicts the Income Tax (Earnings and Pensions) Act 2003, Section 687, which mandates that employers deduct income tax and National Insurance contributions from employees' earnings. Removing this clause creates the implication that such deductions may not necessarily be made or adhered to, which would be illegal.

You have rejected additional cookies. You can change your cookie settings at any time. Accept additional cookies Reject additional cookies View cookies Hide this message Skip to main content Income Tax: introduction Skip contents Overview Income Tax is a tax you pay on your income. You do not have to pay tax on all types of income. This guide is also available in Welsh (Cymraeg) . You pay tax on things like: money you earn from employment profits you make if you’re self-employed, including from services you sell through websites or apps - you can check if you need to tell HMRC about this income some state benefits most pensions, including state pensions, company and personal pensions and retirement annuities rental income (unless you’re a live-in landlord and get less than the Rent a Room Scheme limit) benefits you get from your job income from a trust interest on savings over your savings allowance You do not pay tax on things like: the first £1,000 of income from self-employment - this is your ‘trading allowance’ the first £1,000 of income from property you rent (unless you’re using the Rent a Room Scheme ) income from tax-exempt accounts, like Individual Savings Accounts (ISAs) and National Savings Certificates dividends from company shares under your dividends allowance some state benefits premium bond or National Lottery wins rent you get from a lodger in your house that’s below the Rent a Room Scheme limit If you only occasionally sell items or rent out property (for example through auction websites or short-term rental apps), check if you need to tell HMRC about this income . Income Tax allowances and reliefs Most people in the UK get a Personal Allowance of tax-free income. This is the amount of income you can have before you pay tax. The amount of tax you pay can also be reduced by tax reliefs if you qualify for them. View a printable version of the whole guide Related content Is this page useful? perturbation.justification: The original text states that the executive's salary is subject to deductions required by law, while the changed text omits this clause. The Law Explanation clarifies that this omission contradicts the Income Tax (Earnings and Pensions) Act 2003, Section 687, as it mandates employers to deduct income tax and National Insurance contributions. The absence of this clause in the modified text implies that these legally required deductions may not be adhered to, thus creating a legal contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 2, although generic, generally supports the idea that income tax is required on employment income, reinforcing the contradiction introduced by the omission.

perturbation.original_text: 10.1 The Company intends to comply with the employer pension duties in respect of the Executive in accordance with Part 1 of the Pensions Act 2008.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.1 The Company intends to consider the employer pension duties in respect of the Executive.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'comply with' to 'consider' removes the obligation for the company to adhere to legal pension duties and introduces a perturbation of legal compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the commitment to meet employer pension duties. It replaces the original commitment to 'comply with' to 'consider', which contradicts the Pensions Act 2008, Part 1 (specifically Section 3), which mandates auto-enrolment of eligible employees into a qualifying pension scheme and requires employers to make contributions. By only stating an intent to 'consider' the duties, the employer could argue they are not obligated to fulfil their legal responsibilities under the Act.

perturbation.justification: The original text states the company 'intends to comply' with pension duties, while the changed text replaces this with 'intends to consider,' which significantly weakens the commitment to fulfilling legally mandated pension obligations. The Law Explanation highlights that this contradicts the Pensions Act 2008, Part 1 (specifically Section 3), which requires auto-enrolment and employer contributions. The scraped law snippet provides the context that the Pensions Act 2008 is an official piece of UK legislation.
